steps.title: Move against the gradient
steps.body: Gradient descent starts at a point, looks at the gradient of a function there, and takes a step in the opposite direction. Repeating this can move the point toward a minimum.

steps.title: Direction
steps.prompt: If $\nabla f(x)$ points uphill, which direction does gradient descent step?
choices: Perpendicular to the gradient every time.
explanations: Correct. The negative gradient points in the direction of steepest local decrease.
That would move uphill for a small positive step.
No. Perpendicular motion keeps the first-order value nearly unchanged.

steps.title: Step size
steps.prompt: What can happen when the learning rate is too large?
choices: The iterates can overshoot or zig-zag instead of settling down.
The gradient becomes exactly zero everywhere.
explanations: Correct. Big steps can bounce across the valley or diverge.
No. The learning rate changes the update, not the function itself.
No. The contour plot still describes the same objective.
